claims.text: In the early 1980s engineer Vic Tandy, working at Warwick Labs in the UK, experienced what felt like a textbook ghost encounter — feelings of dread, racing heart, prickling neck, and the glimpse of a gray figure drifting across his peripheral vision — only to discover the haunting was a physical phenomenon caused by a recently installed fan.

evidence.quote:
in the early 1980s vic tandy was
working at warwick labs in the uk where
he designed medical equipment

tandy was working
late when he started to feel strange the
hairs on the back of his neck stood up
he broke into a cold sweat and felt the
sense of dread wash over him

out of the corner of his
eye he caught the glimpse of a gray
figure drifting across the room

claims.rationale: Vic Tandy's 1998 paper in the Journal of the Society for Psychical Research, 'The Ghost in the Machine', is the original source. He worked at Coventry-area medical-equipment maker Warwick (later spelled various ways) and reported the encounter and the diagnosis essentially as told. The host's framing matches the published account closely.

claims.text: Tandy traced the haunting to a fan generating an inaudible standing wave at roughly 19 Hz — below the 20 Hz lower bound of human hearing — that resonated his foil and his eyeball, producing the 'gray figure' as a visual artifact of the eye vibrating at its own resonant frequency.

evidence.quote:
a foil
was locked in a vice and tandy noticed
it was vibrating really quickly even
though nothing was touching it

a candy calculated the
frequency of the sound at about 19 hertz

the range of
human hearing starts at 20 hertz so
tandy's fan wasn't audible it's what's
known as infrasound

the human eye resonates at about 19
hertz so what appeared to be a ghost was
actually tandy's eye vibrating at the
frequency of the fan

claims.rationale: The fan-driven standing wave at ~19 Hz is from Tandy's published account; the human-eye-resonance-at-18.9 Hz figure originates with NASA biomechanics studies (often cited but the figure refers to specific seated-vibration test conditions, not a universal eye property). The 'eye literally vibrated and produced the gray figure' explanation is plausible-but-controversial; some sleep and perception researchers argue the apparition was more likely a anxiety-induced felt-presence percept driven by the infrasound's autonomic effects, not literal eyeball vibration. So the eye-resonance specific causal mechanism is the weak link; the broader 'infrasound caused the experience' is well supported.

claims.text: The frequency band just below 20 Hz has earned the nickname 'fear frequency' because it produces unease in mammals — observed for example in tigers, whose pre-attack roars contain ~18 Hz components that disorient prey, and exploited deliberately by horror-movie sound designers.

evidence.quote:
researchers have discovered that just
before a tiger attacks its roar contains
frequencies at 18 hertz which could
disorient their prey

just
under 20 hertz has been called the fear
frequency for how it affects mammals

claims.rationale: Tiger infrasound research is real (Edward Walsh and colleagues at Boys Town National Research Hospital, ~2000) and tigers do produce sub-20 Hz components in roars; whether these reliably 'disorient prey' is suggestive but not fully established. Horror films do use very-low-frequency drones (e.g., 'Paranormal Activity' soundtrack) deliberately. Nickname 'fear frequency' is informal but accurate to common usage.

claims.text: The 'brown note' — a hypothetical 5–9 Hz frequency rumored to cause involuntary loss of bowel control — has never been demonstrated to exist; MythBusters busted it on Adam Savage with giant speakers, and even high-amplitude lab tests on humans have failed to produce the effect.

evidence.quote:
the brown note is said to occur at
frequencies between five and nine hertz
this is the hypothetical range of sound
that supposedly causes humans to lose
control of their bowels

the brown note
was famously busted by adam savage on
mythbusters

claims.rationale: MythBusters did test the brown note across multiple low-frequency setups in their 2005 episode and no involuntary defecation was produced. The brown note remains a popular myth with no peer-reviewed evidence. Note that what the host calls the 'brown note' (5–9 Hz) is sometimes described instead as Tandy-style infrasound resonance with abdominal organs at ~7 Hz — which can cause discomfort but not bowel loss.

claims.text: U.S. space program tests transmitting infrasound directly into seated subjects' bodies via vibrating cockpit chairs at 0.5–40 Hz and ~160 dB produced nausea, hallucinations, breathing difficulty, and involuntary motor effects — short of producing the brown note, but enough to demonstrate that air-coupled audio testing dramatically underestimates infrasound's biological effects.

evidence.quote:
the united states space program
conducted tests that transferred brown
note and other frequencies directly to
subjects bodies by vibrating cockpit
chairs

test frequencies range from
0.5 hertz to 40 hertz and power levels
of 160 decibels

test subjects
experience nausea hallucinations
difficulty breathing and involuntary
motor functions

claims.rationale: NASA and USAF did conduct human-subject vibration testing in the 1960s–70s for spacecraft and aircraft seat ergonomics (e.g., NASA Technical Report TM X-2620 series); the 0.5–40 Hz range and reported subject effects are consistent with that literature. 160 dB whole-body coupled vibration would indeed produce serious physiological effects. The framing is broadly accurate but specific test program details are loose.

dependencies.rationale: Explains why MythBusters' air-coupled test failed even if direct-coupling could produce effects.

claims.text: Roughly 20% of people living near large wind-turbine farms report symptoms ranging from concentration difficulty to extreme stress and migraines (sometimes called 'wind turbine syndrome') — a phenomenon the wind industry's own ~20 industry-funded studies have failed to confirm.

evidence.quote:
now not everyone but a
little over 20 of people who live near
large wind turbine farms report all
kinds of negative effects

there have been
about 20 studies done on wind farms but
they found no link between the turbines
and people getting sick who conducted
these studies
the wind turbine companies

claims.rationale: Wind Turbine Syndrome was popularized by Nina Pierpont's self-published book; peer-reviewed reviews (NHMRC Australia, Health Canada Wind Turbine Noise study) have found no consistent dose-response relationship between turbine infrasound and illness, but they did find higher annoyance and sleep disturbance in those bothered by visible turbines. The 'industry-funded' framing is misleading — the major reviews were government-funded. So the host's '20% sick + only industry studies say no' is one-sided.

claims.text: The most dangerous frequencies to humans are around 7 Hz — close to both the median brain alpha rhythm and the resonant frequency of major organs — at which sufficient amplitude can directly affect the central nervous system, causing convulsions, vomiting, organ rupture, and death.

evidence.quote:
the most dangerous frequencies to humans
are at about seven hertz this is the
median alpha rhythm of the brain and the
resonant frequency of many of the body's
organs

at high enough
volume these sounds can directly affect
the central nervous system causing panic
convulsions vomiting and with long
enough exposure organ rupture and death

claims.rationale: The ~7 Hz figure aligns with whole-body resonance literature (the abdominal cavity resonates near 4–8 Hz) and EEG alpha is roughly 8–12 Hz. However, the 'organ rupture and death' framing requires extreme amplitudes and is mostly drawn from Vladimir Gavreau's largely unreplicated 1960s claims. Modern acoustics literature is much more cautious; the lethal-infrasound mythos is partly inflated.

claims.text: Russian-born French scientist Vladimir Gavreau pioneered infrasonic weapon development for the French military starting in 1957, after diagnosing a mystery industrial illness as low-frequency air-conditioning noise — and according to reports a researcher died with internal organs 'mashed into amorphous jelly' during prototype tests.

evidence.quote:
one of the most well-known inventors
of infrasonic weapons was a russian-born
french scientist named vladimir govrow

according to reports one of the
researchers died instantly and guerrero
wrote his internal organs mashed into an
amorphous jelly by the vibrations

claims.rationale: Vladimir Gavreau (1903–1967) was real, ran a Marseille electroacoustics lab, and his accidentally-stumbled-into infrasound illness story (employees sickened by an air-conditioning duct) is documented in 1960s reports and a 1968 Science Digest piece. However, the 'researcher mashed to jelly' detail is a much later embellishment that does not appear in Gavreau's own published work; it spread through 1970s-80s pop-science magazines. Gavreau's actual experiments produced discomfort and disorientation, not death. Mid-low directTruth: the historical existence and program are real, the deadly-prototype anecdote is mostly myth.

claims.text: The U.S. Navy has fielded ultrasound-based sonic weapons (e.g., the Long Range Acoustic Device) operationally to repel pirates — establishing that sonic weapons are not theoretical but a real, deployed military technology.

evidence.quote:
ultrasound is such an
effective weapon that has been used by
the us navy to repel pirates

claims.rationale: The LRAD (Long Range Acoustic Device) by Genasys is real, used by U.S. Navy and other agencies, and was famously deployed against Somali pirates aboard the cruise ship Seabourn Spirit in 2005. However, LRAD is not strictly 'ultrasound' — it produces highly directional audible-band sound (often above 150 dB) plus warning tones. The host's category 'ultrasound' is technically wrong, but the broader 'deployed sonic weapon' claim is true.

claims.text: Havana Syndrome — a real, ongoing medical mystery — has affected American intelligence officers and diplomats with symptoms including migraines, dizziness, cognitive disruption, and concussion-like brain tissue changes, beginning at the U.S. embassy in Havana in 2016 and spreading worldwide.

evidence.quote:
a strange illness has been afflicting
american intelligence officers and
diplomats in cuba and it's now known as
havana syndrome

the first cases of havana syndrome
were reported in 2016 when canadian and
american officials arrived in cuba

brain scans
showed tissue damage that's normally
seen in people with concussions after
being in a car accident

claims.rationale: Havana Syndrome is real as a *clinical phenomenon* (Anomalous Health Incidents). The 2016 Havana onset, evacuation under Obama administration, and worldwide spread are all documented. The 'concussion-like brain damage' claim was prominent in Smith et al. JAMA 2018 but later challenged — a NIH 2024 study found no consistent imaging differences between AHI patients and controls, and a 2023 ODNI assessment concluded most cases are unlikely to have a foreign-adversary cause. So 'real symptoms' yes, 'concussion-like brain damage as established fact' no.

claims.text: Investigators have proposed three competing technical explanations for Havana Syndrome — a sonic weapon, high-energy microwaves, and (per a National Academies report) directed pulsed radio-frequency energy — without any of them being conclusively confirmed.
entities: organization:national academies of science engineering and medicine

evidence.quote:
according to investigators the illness
could be the result of a sonic weapon or
exposure to high-energy microwaves
another study done by the national
academies of science engineering and
medicine said that directed pulsed radio
frequency energy could be responsible

claims.rationale: The 2020 National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine report does cite directed pulsed RF (microwave) energy as the most plausible mechanism for the Havana cluster. Sonic-weapon and conventional-microwave hypotheses have also been seriously discussed in peer-reviewed and intelligence-community publications. None has been confirmed. The host's tri-hypothesis summary is fair.

claims.text: Many U.S. intelligence officers privately suspect Russia is behind Havana Syndrome attacks — though no public evidence supports the attribution and the Russian government denies involvement.

evidence.quote:
many intelligence officers are quietly
pointing the finger at russia

claims.rationale: Russian GRU Unit 29155 has been alleged in major outlets (60 Minutes, The Insider, Der Spiegel, 2024) to be linked to AHIs, and some former intel officers do publicly attribute the cluster to Russia. However, the U.S. Intelligence Community's official 2023 assessment was that it is 'very unlikely' a foreign adversary is responsible for most cases — so the official IC view contradicts the 'quiet attribution to Russia' the host reports. Mixed and disputed.
